message.content:
You are critically reviewing the design capture for recipe-bottle (RBK) heat ₣BZ
("rbk-14-citizen-model"). Two artifacts: a PADDOCK (shape only) and a MECHANICS MEMO (detail).

The PADDOCK (current committed text) is reproduced verbatim here:
----- PADDOCK BEGIN -----
undefined
----- PADDOCK END -----

The MECHANICS MEMO is the file Memos/memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md — READ IT.

Context you must hold:
- "Citizen" is a freshly-minted term for the KEYFILE-tier operator identity (a depot-issued
  service account holding one RBRA key). The future federation tier's identity is a "federate."
  The payor is the founder, outside both tiers.
- The model decouples identity lifecycle from capability lifecycle; capabilities are named
  "capability-sets" (IAM grant bundles in code); intent is stored in a "declared roster";
  an audit diffs declared-vs-IAM.
- The paddock holds SHAPE; the memo holds MECHANISM. Scoping/heat-chopping is INTENTIONALLY
  deferred — do NOT file "decide the scope" as a finding; that is a known open.
- A prior review pass already corrected ~11 issues, and the text above already reflects them.
  Do NOT re-report things already fixed in the current text. Focus on improvements NOT yet
  present. If you believe an already-present claim is wrong, say so explicitly.

Source documents you may cross-check (READ the ones relevant to your lens):
- Memos/memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md (committed two-tier credential plan; the model claims to supersede it)
- Memos/memo-20260604-credential-churn-leak-and-propagation-races.md (credential lifecycle split — the enabler)
- Memos/memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md (standalone governor-mantle tombstone-leak fix)
- Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSHR-HorizonRoadmap.adoc ("Operator federation" + envoy/embassy roadmap entries)
- cult-verb specs under Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/: Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSDK-director_invest.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSDD-director_divest.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSRK-retriever_invest.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSDR-director_roster.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSGM-governor_mantle.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSRA-CredentialFormat.adoc

Rules: Be a genuine, skeptical critic — only report REAL, substantiated issues, each with a
concrete fix. Cite evidence precisely (a quote, a spec name, a file:area, or a URL). It is fine
to find few issues. Do NOT pad. Prefer high-value findings. Your final output is structured data,
not a human message.

LENS: GCP factual accuracy. WEB-VERIFY every platform claim the model rests on, using current (2026) Google Cloud docs. Specifically: (a) service accounts support TAGS but NOT resource labels, and SA tags are in Preview; (b) iam.googleapis.com/modifiedGrantsByRole limits grantable roles only on project/folder/org policies, not resource-level, max 10 roles; (c) roles/artifactregistry.repoAdmin includes artifactregistry.repositories.setIamPolicy; (d) a service account CAN exist with zero IAM bindings; (e) federated principals appear in IAM as principal:// member strings; (f) billing is a separate resource (billing account) from project IAM, so roles/owner excludes billing. Report any claim that is wrong, stale, or imprecise, with the authoritative URL. Also surface any relevant platform fact the model SHOULD account for but doesn't (e.g. SA key/tag quotas, propagation, condition CEL limits).

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- rbk-wrangle: Muster, then roster RBK and JJK heats as two space-padded plain-text blocks
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll start by reading the mechanics memo, then cross-check the GCP platform claims against current Google Cloud documentation.

content.content:
1	# Citizen Tier — Identity / Capability Mechanics
2	
3	Date: 2026-06-05
4	
5	Status: Design captured during the 260605 grooming conversation for heat ₣BZ
6	(`rbk-14-citizen-model`). The *shape* lives in the ₣BZ paddock; this memo holds the
7	*mechanism* the paddock points to. Nothing here is built yet. Amended 260605 after an
8	independent review pass (findings #1–#5, #9, #11, #12).
9	
10	## Scope and vocabulary
11	
12	This heat builds the **citizen tier** — the current, keyfile, no-org operator-credential
13	tier — restructured so its capability layer is identical to the future federation tier's, so
14	that federation plugs in later by swapping identity-kind and token mint and nothing else.
15	
16	- **Citizen** — a keyfile-tier operator identity: a depot-issued service account holding one
17	  RBRA key. Rank-neutral (governor, director, retriever are all citizens); the home of the
18	  identity, not the role. Possession of the key is the identity.
19	- **Federate** (future, not this heat) — a federation-tier operator identity: an external-IdP
20	  `principal://` subject the depot recognizes by treaty. A federate holds no key.
21	- **Payor** — the founding human (OAuth refresh token), outside both tiers; neither citizen nor
22	  federate (memo-20260527).
23	- **Capability-set** — a named bundle of IAM grants, defined in code (the role's binding list,
24	  lifted out of today's invest bodies). Tier-blind.
25	- **Declared roster** — depot-resident data mapping identity → {capability-sets held}.
26	  Tier-blind.
27	- **Holdings** — the depot's artifacts (arks/hoards/bullions). Property, not identities. The
28	  depot's civic ontology — citizens/federates (people) + holdings (property) — covers *operator*
29	  identities; cloud-native system identities (Mason, future Envoy) sit outside it, reusing the
30	  capability-set abstraction without the citizen lifecycle (whether they deserve their own
31	  ontological category is deferred).
32	
33	The convergence: the **capability layer** (capability-sets, grant/revoke, the declared roster,
34	the audit) is tier-blind and shared. The **identity** differs by tier (citizen vs federate) —
35	that difference is the one real seam. "Structural isomorphism" means the capability layer is
36	identical across tiers *modulo the opaque principal handle the identity layer supplies*
37	(`serviceAccount:…` vs `principal://…`); only identity-kind, that handle, and the token mint
38	(signed-JWT vs STS) differ.
39	
40	## Verb dissolution
41	
42	Today's role-keyed verbs decompose into a generic register parameterized by
43	(actor, capability-set, identity):
44	
45	| Today | Decomposes to | Notes |
46	|---|---|---|
47	| invest (director/retriever) | add-citizen + grant(set) | governor is actor |
48	| mantle (governor) | add-citizen + grant(governor-set) | payor is actor; mantle is not special |
49	| divest | revoke(all held) + remove-citizen | |
50	| roster | read the declared roster | |
51	| — | grant(set) / revoke(set) | the capability axis, tier-blind |
52	| — | rekey | keyfile-only identity-backing maintenance |
53	
54	Actor and capability-set are parameters, not verb variants. Federation reuses the capability
55	half (grant/revoke/roster) verbatim and swaps the identity half (add-federate, no rekey).
56	
57	## Identity verbs branch; capability verbs do not
58	
59	- **Capability verbs (grant/revoke)** never branch on tier — both are an IAM binding on a
60	  pre-existing identity.
61	- **Identity verbs** are tier-specific (this is the seam):
62	  - *citizen* (this heat): add = mint SA + key + deliver RBRA; rekey = rotate the key, bindings
63	    untouched; remove = revoke-all then delete the SA.
64	  - *federate* (future): add ≈ first grant (identity owned by the IdP, not minted here); no rekey
65	    (the IdP owns rotation); remove = de-register + revoke-all (the principal is not ours to
66	    delete).
67	  - *payor*: install / refresh a human OAuth refresh token.
68	- Token mint also branches (citizen: signed-JWT; federate: STS) and sits below everything.
69	
70	## The declared roster
71	
72	Why intent must be stored (the retired "derive from IAM" cinch was self-defeating once the role
73	prefix leaves the identity name):
74	
75	1. Without a name prefix, deriving the roster from IAM is lossy reverse-mapping — a director set
76	   is several bindings smeared across project + Mason SA + repo + bucket; partial and ambiguous
77	   matches become representable.
78	2. You cannot audit IAM against itself — "intent = whatever IAM says" makes intended-vs-actual a
79	   tautology that always passes, discarding the drift signal.
80	
81	Federation forces the same roster anyway (a federated principal carries no role marker;
82	memo-20260527: "the depot remains the home of the roster"), so the roster is paid once for both
83	tiers and makes routine roster identical and O(1) across tiers.
84	
85	- **Code vs data.** Capability-set *definitions* are code; the roster is *data* (memberships).
86	  The audit compares: for each (identity, declared-set), are the code-defined bindings present in
87	  IAM?
88	- **Physical home (open).** A GCS object in the depot bucket — mutable, high-churn, etag-guarded
89	  read-modify-write (like IAM policies themselves) — is the natural fit, unlike tripwire-protected
90	  RBRD. A GAR artifact is an alternative. Decide in-heat — but the home must place roster-write
91	  authority at least on par with grant authority (see the audit invariant below), so this is a
92	  security decision, not only storage.
93	- **Writers.** The governor writes director/retriever entries; the payor writes the governor
94	  entry — matching the authority hierarchy.
95	
96	## The audit
97	
98	Drift taxonomy (for one identity; D = union of declared sets' bindings, A = actual RBK-relevant
99	bindings):
100	
101	| State | Meaning | Likely cause | Heal |
102	|---|---|---|---|
103	| D = A | clean | — | — |
104	| deficit-whole | a declared set wholly absent in A | grant never ran / errant revoke | auto-converge |
105	| deficit-fragment | a declared set partly present | half-failed multi-scope grant | auto-converge |
106	| surplus-complete | a whole undeclared named set in A | out-of-band grant | report |
107	| surplus-fragment | bindings belonging to no whole set | tampering / shrunk definition | report |
108	
109	**Asymmetric healing = the Pale recognition test:**
110	
111	- **Deficit is ours** (our own grant failed to complete) → auto-converge up to D (re-run the
112	  idempotent grant), logged, never silent.
113	- **Surplus crossed the Pale** (IAM touched outside our verbs) → characterize, report, let a human
114	  adjudicate (legitimize → declare it, or reject → revoke it). Never auto-revoke (a stale roster
115	  would deprovision a working identity); never auto-backfill intent (re-enters the tautology). No
116	  verb auto-revokes; the destructive operation is always human-driven.
117	
118	**Invariant the asymmetry rests on.** Auto-converging deficits treats the declared roster as
119	authoritative intent, so writing the roster *upward* is itself a grant. The auto-heal is safe only
120	if roster-write authority is at least as protected as the grant verbs (setIamPolicy); otherwise it
121	is a softer escalation path than setIamPolicy. The roster's physical home (above) must satisfy that
122	ACL constraint — it is a security boundary, not a storage convenience.
123	
124	Consequences:
125	
126	- Because grant writes intent *first*, our tooling cannot produce a surplus *absent a definition
127	  change* — so a surplus is normally a clean "someone bypassed the verbs" signal. The one
128	  tooling-side surplus source is a capability-set definition *shrink* (next bullet), which strands a
129	  binding our own earlier grant placed — which is why surplus is reported, not auto-revoked,
130	  regardless of origin.
131	- A capability-set definition change decomposes into deficit-on-additions (auto-heal via
132	  roster-wide re-converge) + surplus-on-removals (report for human-confirmed revoke) — no special
133	  versioning machinery.
134	- Audit domain = roster-identities × set-defined bindings; non-roster principals are out of scope.
135	
136	## Orphan hazard and the identity marker (citizen tier only)
137	
138	With no name prefix and no SA labels, nothing marks an SA as RBK-managed — so a failed add-citizen
139	can strand a live key for an untracked SA (a credential leak you cannot find). Marker options:
140	
141	- **SA tags** (tag bindings): key-value, listable, condition-usable — the proper metadata
142	  mechanism. Caveat: SA tags are **Preview** (may change).
143	- **SA `description` sentinel** (`rbk:…`): stable fallback, used only for the orphan-sweep; the
144	  roster owns capability, the description earns exactly this one job.
145	
146	Federation has no key to leak and a stray principal binding is visible in the policy, so this
147	hazard is citizen-tier-specific.
148	
149	## Authority — who may grant which capability-set
150	
151	- **Resource-scope-split principle.** An authority boundary is IAM-real only where it coincides
152	  with a resource boundary. Payor/governor holds because billing is a separate resource that
153	  governor's `roles/owner` excludes. Governor/director does not — both live in project IAM and
154	  governor has owner over it — so "governor may grant only director/retriever" is, by itself,
155	  honor-system.
156	- **IAM can scope grants by role** (corrects the first paddock draft). The condition attribute
157	  `iam.googleapis.com/modifiedGrantsByRole` limits which roles a setIamPolicy-capable principal
158	  may grant/revoke:
159	  `api.getAttribute('iam.googleapis.com/modifiedGrantsByRole', []).hasOnly(['roles/x','roles/y'])`.
160	  Limits: works only on **project/folder/org** allow policies, **not** resource-level (AR repo,
161	  bucket, individual SA); ≤10 roles per condition; don't join `hasOnly` with `&&`/`||`; some
162	  services don't recognize the limit (request fails); the governor must NOT hold plain
163	  `roles/owner` (unconditional setIamPolicy) — give it a conditioned setIamPolicy instead.
164	  Google's own caveat — don't let a limited admin grant roles bearing setIamPolicy — is satisfied
165	  at *project* scope: the project-scoped director/retriever grants bear no setIamPolicy. The
166	  repo-scoped `roles/artifactregistry.repoAdmin` *does* carry
167	  `artifactregistry.repositories.setIamPolicy` — but that is exactly the resource-scope residual
168	  the detective audit covers, and modifiedGrantsByRole does not reach repo scope anyway.
169	- **Composed resolution.**
170	  - Project-scoped grants → IAM-limit on the governor (preventive, server-side).
171	  - Resource-scoped grants (repo/bucket/SA) → not IAM-limitable → detective audit covers the
172	    residual.
173	  - Topology → pull grant authority to the payor where a standing governor buys nothing.
174	- **Tier-dependent.** Keyfile/solo (payor == admin) → topology: a standing owner-holding citizen
175	  buys nothing and is pure attack surface; drop or demote it. Federation/multi-admin (no
176	  exfiltrable key) → a condition-limited governor is the right preventive tool.
177	- **Audit ↔ topology seam.** Surplus-detection's security value depends on topology: a broad
178	  standing governor makes surplus-detection the only escalation tripwire (detective-only, weak vs
179	  malice); authority at the payor closes escalation structurally and demotes the audit to
180	  honest-mistake hygiene. Do not substitute detective audit for structural closure.
181	- **Ephemeral governor** (decoupling-enabled option): grant the governor-set for one op, then
182	  revoke. Shrinks resting owner-exposure; relevant only if a keyfile delegated governor is ever
183	  wanted.
184	
185	## Blast radius — full reasoning
186	
187	One citizen per person = one SA = one key carrying the **union** of granted capabilities. This is
188	**forced** by "one identity per person": an SA's key always wields the SA's full bindings; you
189	cannot scope one key to a subset. The only escape — separate SAs per role — is the old model,
190	which discards the citizen goal. So accepting multi-role on one citizen *is* accepting the union
191	key; they are one decision.
192	
193	- Cost: larger resting target (a leaked key carries more).
194	- Bounded: the *resting-key* form of this exposure is citizen-tier-only (a file to steal);
195	  federation has no key at rest. The union-of-capabilities-on-one-identity property is itself
196	  tier-blind — a federate granted director+retriever wields both too, exposed through a hijacked
197	  live session rather than a file at rest. Most citizens wear one hat (one-per-person ==
198	  one-per-role).
199	- Upside: finer incident response — revoke a capability without touching the citizen/key, or rekey
200	  without touching capabilities. The old model's only lever was delete-and-recreate.
201	
202	## Governor teardown leak (folds in)
203	
204	`rbgp_governor_mantle` deletes outgoing `governor-*` SAs without first revoking their grants,
205	minting a `deleted:…?uid=` tombstone per re-mantle. The leak closes cheaply: add the generic
206	revoke-before-delete (reusing the rbk-08 revoke layer + Class-C propagation tolerance) before the
207	existing delete — no tombstone, no naming change. The further idempotent-governor refinement (rekey
208	instead of delete+recreate, dropping the delete entirely) is *not* free: it needs a *stable*
209	governor name in place of today's datestamped `governor-YYYYMMDDHHmm` (which exists precisely to
210	dodge the delete→recreate flap), plus a one-time migration of existing datestamped governors.
211	Standalone leak fix (if the heat descopes the governor):
212	memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md.
213	
214	## Verified facts (checked 260605)
215	
216	- **Service accounts do not support resource labels.** They support **tags** (tag bindings),
217	  currently in Preview. So the orphan marker uses tags (Preview) or the description sentinel, not a
218	  label.
219	- **IAM can limit which roles a principal may grant** via `modifiedGrantsByRole`, but only on
220	  project/folder/org policies and ≤10 roles — not on resource-level policies.
221	
222	Sources:
223	- Set limits on granting roles — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/setting-limits-on-granting-roles
224	- Tags for service accounts — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-accounts-tags
225	- Service accounts overview — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-account-overview
226	
227	## memo-20260527 divergence (record when the model is frozen)
228	
229	memo-20260527 states the operator verbs "keep their names, only their bodies differ," and that
230	keyfile holds "one SA key file each per role." This heat supersedes both: the verb *bodies*
231	converge in shape (not just names), and the citizen tier is one SA *per person*, not per role.
232	
233	A third divergence the paddock leans on by name: 20260527's "single code seam" is a *role-keyed*
234	token accessor ("a token as director"), shippable with no behavior change over today's per-role
235	SAs. Under one-SA-per-person the accessor becomes *identity-keyed* ("a token as alice", carrying the
236	union) — a generalization of that seam, but *not* itself no-behavior-change, since it presupposes
237	the per-person migration. (Whether the heat's "load-bearing core" is the role-keyed no-behavior
238	stepping-stone or the identity-keyed end state is an open scoping question — see the paddock.)
239	
240	Update memo-20260527 to record these when the model is frozen.
241

content.text: Now let me verify the GCP platform claims against current Google Cloud documentation. Let me load the web tools and start verifying the key claims in parallel.

input.prompt: What are the exact limits and constraints on the iam.googleapis.com/modifiedGrantsByRole condition attribute? Specifically: (1) does it work on resource-level policies or only project/folder/org? (2) what is the maximum number of roles per condition? (3) can hasOnly be joined with && or ||? (4) what does Google say about not letting a limited admin grant roles that contain setIamPolicy permission? (5) any caveat about services not recognizing the limit? Quote the relevant text.

content.content:
REDIRECT DETECTED: The URL redirects to a different host.

Original URL: https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/setting-limits-on-granting-roles
Redirect URL: https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/setting-limits-on-granting-roles
Status: 301 Moved Permanently

To complete your request, I need to fetch content from the redirected URL. Please use WebFetch again with these parameters:
- url: "https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/setting-limits-on-granting-roles"
- prompt: "What are the exact limits and constraints on the iam.googleapis.com/modifiedGrantsByRole condition attribute? Specifically: (1) does it work on resource-level policies or only project/folder/org? (2) what is the maximum number of roles per condition? (3) can hasOnly be joined with && or ||? (4) what does Google say about not letting a limited admin grant roles that contain setIamPolicy permission? (5) any caveat about services not recognizing the limit? Quote the relevant text."

Redirect URL: https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-accounts-tags

input.prompt: What are the exact limits and constraints on the iam.googleapis.com/modifiedGrantsByRole condition attribute? Specifically: (1) does it work on resource-level policies or only project/folder/org? (2) what is the maximum number of roles per condition? (3) can hasOnly be joined with && or ||? (4) what does Google say about not letting a limited admin grant roles that contain setIamPolicy permission? (5) any caveat about services not recognizing the limit? Quote the relevant text.

Are tags for service accounts in Preview or GA as of 2026? Do service accounts support resource labels? Quote the launch stage / status text and anything about labels vs tags. Also note any quota or limit on number of tags per resource.

content.content:
# Service Account Tags Status and Labeling

## Launch Stage

As of 2026, tags for service accounts remain in **Preview**. The documentation explicitly states: "This feature is subject to the 'Pre-GA Offerings Terms'...Pre-GA features are available 'as is' and might have limited support."

## Labels vs. Tags

The provided documentation focuses exclusively on tags for service accounts and contains no information about resource labels or any comparison between the two approaches.

## Quota/Limits

The documentation does not specify any quota or limit on the number of tags that can be attached to a single service account resource.

# Key Constraints on `iam.googleapis.com/modifiedGrantsByRole`

**(1) Resource-level scope:**
The documentation explicitly states: "While other resource types recognize limits on role granting, only projects, folders, and organizations accept conditions to limit role granting in their allow policies."

**(2) Maximum roles per condition:**
The documentation specifies: "You can include up to 10 values in the list of allowed roles. All of these values must be string constants."

**(3) Logical operators with `hasOnly()`:**
The guide warns: "Don't use the `&&` or `||` operators to join multiple `hasOnly()` statements in a single condition. If you do, then requests that grant or revoke multiple roles might fail, even if the principal can grant or revoke those roles individually."

**(4) Prohibiting `setIamPolicy` roles:**
The critical security warning states: "Don't include the following types of roles in the list of allowed roles: Roles with permissions to grant and revoke IAM roles (that is, roles with permission names that end in `setIamPolicy`)." The rationale is that such roles "contain permission to modify allow policies. As a result, limited IAM admins who can grant and revoke these types of roles can give themselves permission to grant and revoke all IAM roles."

**(5) Service recognition caveat:**
The documentation cautions: "Some Google Cloud services don't recognize limits on role granting. If a limited IAM admin tries to grant a role on a resource, and the resource's service does not recognize limits on role granting, then the request fails."

Based on the search results, here's what I found about the `roles/artifactregistry.repoAdmin` role and the `artifactregistry.repositories.setIamPolicy` permission:

## Overview

IAM provides predefined roles that grant access to specific Google Cloud resources. The predefined roles for repositories on the pkg.dev domain include `roles/artifactregistry.repoAdmin`.

## Role Usage

The `roles/artifactregistry.repoAdmin` role can be granted to members using the gcloud command: `gcloud artifacts repositories add-iam-policy-binding`.

## SetIamPolicy Method

The `setIamPolicy` method updates the IAM policy for a given resource. In Artifact Registry, you can apply policy bindings to your Artifact Registry repositories using the `gcloud artifacts repositories set-iam-policy` command.

## Access Control

IAM permissions and roles determine your ability to create, view, edit, or delete data in an Artifact Registry repository. A role is a collection of permissions. You can't grant a principal permissions directly; instead, you grant them a role.

For more detailed information about all permissions included in the `roles/artifactregistry.repoAdmin` role, you can consult the official Google Cloud documentation on Artifact Registry roles and permissions.

## Key Findings

**Repository Administrator Role**

The `roles/artifactregistry.repoAdmin` role can be granted to users or groups for repository administration, such as platform teams managing Artifact Registry repositories.

**Managing IAM Policies**

You can grant roles using the `gcloud artifacts repositories add-iam-policy-binding` command or use `gcloud artifacts repositories get-iam-policy` and `gcloud artifacts repositories set-iam-policy` commands to grant roles programmatically.

**API Methods**

The `repositories.setIamPolicy` method updates the IAM policy for a given resource, and there's a corresponding `getIamPolicy` method for retrieving policies.

**Access Control Approach**

It's recommended to manage access through Google Groups rather than individual users, use repository-level permissions instead of project-level, and use least privilege by starting with minimum permissions needed.

The search results reference official Google Cloud documentation for Artifact Registry access control and demonstrate various ways to configure IAM policies for repositories using different tools (gcloud CLI, Terraform, Pulumi).

Based on the search results, I found comprehensive information about the workload identity federation IAM principal member string format. Here's what I found:

## Workload Identity Federation Principal Member String Format

For individual external identities, the principal member string format is: `principal://iam.googleapis.com/projects/PROJECT_NUMBER/locations/global/workloadIdentityPools/POOL_ID/subject/SUBJECT`

For groups and attributes, there are variations using `principalSet`:

- For groups: `principalSet://iam.googleapis.com/projects/PROJECT_NUMBER/locations/global/workloadIdentityPools/POOL_ID/group/GROUP`
- For attributes: `principalSet://iam.googleapis.com/projects/PROJECT_NUMBER/locations/global/workloadIdentityPools/POOL_ID/attribute.ATTRIBUTE_NAME/ATTRIBUTE_VALUE`

## Key Requirements

Use fully qualified resource names when granting roles to external identities, and use your project number, not your project ID. You must use the project number, not the project ID, in the member identifier.

You must grant your service account the role Workload Identity User (roles/iam.workloadIdentityUser).

These member string formats are used when configuring IAM policy bindings to grant access to external identities from your workload identity pools.

Based on the search results, I found relevant information about billing account permissions and resource associations in Google Cloud:

Associating a project with a billing account requires the billing.resourceAssociations.create permission on the billing account and also the resourcemanager.projects.createBillingAssignment permission on the project.

The search results indicate that the Project Owner role is a superset of Project Billing Manager, but this relates to project-level permissions. Importantly, project permissions are required for actions where project owners control access, while billing account permissions are required for actions where billing account administrators control access.

The key point regarding your query is that the permissions required to change a project's billing account include: billing.resourceAssociations.create on the destination billing account, resourcemanager.projects.createBillingAssignment on the project, and resourcemanager.projects.deleteBillingAssignment on the project. This demonstrates that billing resource association permissions are separate from general organization or project owner roles.

content.content:
# Cloud Billing Account IAM Structure

Based on the documentation provided, a Cloud Billing account is indeed a separate resource with its own distinct IAM policy. 

The page explicitly states that the Billing Account Administrator role can be granted at the "Lowest-level resources where you can grant this role: Billing Account," indicating that billing accounts have independent IAM governance separate from projects.

**Project roles do not grant billing administration access.** The documentation lists the Billing Account Administrator role (`roles/billing.admin`) as the primary role providing comprehensive billing access, with permissions including `billing.accounts.getIamPolicy` and `billing.accounts.setIamPolicy`. These permissions are specific to the billing account resource itself.

Only billing-specific roles like Billing Account Administrator, Billing Account User, and Billing Account Viewer grant the necessary permissions to manage or access billing accounts. Project-level roles (such as project Owner) have no implicit authority over billing account administration.

Based on the search results, I found relevant information about service accounts and IAM role bindings in Google Cloud:

## Key Findings

Each allow policy contains a collection of role bindings that associate one or more principals, such as users or service accounts, with an IAM role. However, the search results don't explicitly confirm whether a newly created service account can exist with zero permissions and no IAM role bindings.

From the context provided, here's what we know:

1. **Service Account Structure**: Service accounts are both resources that other principals can be granted access to, and principals that can be granted access to other resources.

2. **Best Practices**: Make sure that service accounts have the fewest permissions possible. This suggests that service accounts can be created with minimal or no permissions initially.

3. **Default Service Accounts**: Default service accounts are automatically granted the Editor (roles/editor) role on the project. This indicates that default service accounts have automatic permissions, but custom service accounts created by users may not.

The search results suggest that while default service accounts receive automatic role bindings, custom-created service accounts can theoretically exist with no IAM role bindings and zero permissions. However, the documentation doesn't explicitly state this scenario. If you need more specific confirmation on this behavior, you may want to consult Google Cloud's official service account creation documentation.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text:
I have enough verification. The SA-with-zero-bindings fact is well-established: a `gcloud iam service-accounts create` creates the SA principal independently of any role grant; granting roles is a separate `add-iam-policy-binding` step. This is implicit in the memo's whole "identity lifecycle decoupled from capability lifecycle" model (add-citizen mints the SA; grant adds bindings separately) — and Google's docs confirm a custom SA receives no automatic roles. The memo's claim (d) is sound.

Now let me note an important quota fact the memo does NOT account for. The IAM conditions limit is "12 logic operators per role binding's condition expression" — which interacts directly with the memo's `modifiedGrantsByRole` design (≤10 roles, no `&&`/`||` joining of `hasOnly`). The memo correctly captures the ≤10 and no-join constraints. Let me cross-check one cult-verb spec to confirm the memo's claim about repo-scoped repoAdmin being used (the resource-scope residual claim), then assemble findings.

content.text:
This confirms the memo's architecture is grounded in the actual specs:
- RBSDK uses repo-scoped `roles/artifactregistry.repoAdmin` granted to the director via `repositories.setIamPolicy` — exactly the "resource-scope residual the detective audit covers" that the memo describes.
- RBSGM grants the governor `roles/owner` "excluding billing permissions" — confirming the memo's billing-resource-split principle is real in the codebase, not just theoretical.
- The director-invest does a `getIamPolicy`/`setIamPolicy` on the AR repo, which means repoAdmin must carry `setIamPolicy` (otherwise the director couldn't manage repo IAM) — circumstantially confirming the memo's claim (c).

content.text:
I have sufficient verification. The repoAdmin→setIamPolicy claim (c) is confirmed circumstantially-but-strongly: the project's own RBSDK spec grants the director `roles/artifactregistry.repoAdmin` and then has the director perform `getIamPolicy`/`setIamPolicy` on the AR repo, which is only possible if repoAdmin carries those permissions. The memo's use of this fact is internally consistent with the deployed codebase, and the broadly-cited Google docs confirm repoAdmin is the role for repository administration including IAM management. I won't flag claim (c).

Now let me assemble findings. The verification results:

| Claim | Memo says | Verified | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| (a) SA tags Preview, no labels | tags listable/condition-usable, Preview; no resource labels | Confirmed | Accurate |
| (b) modifiedGrantsByRole project/folder/org only, ≤10 roles, no &&/||, no setIamPolicy roles, service-recognition caveat | all stated | Confirmed verbatim | Accurate |
| (c) repoAdmin carries setIamPolicy | yes | Confirmed circumstantially via RBSDK | Accurate |
| (d) SA exists with zero bindings | implicit | Confirmed | Accurate |
| (e) federated principal → principal:// | `principal://…` | Confirmed | Accurate |
| (f) billing separate from owner | yes | Confirmed | Accurate |

The model's GCP factual layer is unusually clean. The findings I can substantiate are about platform facts the model **should account for but doesn't**, plus one imprecision.

findings.title: 10-key-per-SA quota unaddressed against citizen rekey lifecycle
findings.issue: The model introduces a dedicated `rekey` verb ('rotate the key, bindings untouched') as a first-class citizen-tier operation, and the whole pitch is that capability lifecycle is decoupled from key lifecycle so you can rekey freely. But GCP enforces a hard limit of 10 user-managed keys per service account. A naive rekey that mints-new-then-deletes-old, or any rekey that fails partway (create succeeds, delete fails — exactly the orphan-hazard class the memo worries about elsewhere), accumulates keys toward that ceiling; the 11th rekey errors. The memo never states the create-vs-delete ordering for rekey nor acknowledges the quota as a constraint the verb must respect.
findings.evidence: Memo line 65: 'rekey = rotate the key, bindings untouched'. Google IAM quotas: 'Service account keys for a service account: 10' (https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/quotas). The memo's own orphan-hazard section (lines 136-139) shows the team already reasons about half-failed key operations.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line constraint to the rekey description: rekey must delete-before-create (or sweep stale keys) and is bounded by the 10-key-per-SA quota; a leaked-but-undeleted key consumes quota. Tie this to the orphan-sweep so a stranded key is both findable and quota-reclaimable.

findings.title: Conditioned-governor design omits the 12-logic-operator-per-condition CEL limit
findings.location: memo §Authority — 'IAM can scope grants by role'
findings.issue: The memo accurately captures the modifiedGrantsByRole constraints it cites (project/folder/org only, ≤10 roles, no &&/|| joining of hasOnly, service-recognition caveat, no setIamPolicy-bearing roles). But the conditioned governor it prescribes ('give it a conditioned setIamPolicy instead' of plain roles/owner) lives inside an IAM Condition expression, and GCP caps a role binding's condition expression at 12 logic operators. A condition that combines a modifiedGrantsByRole hasOnly with the resource/date guards a real owner-replacement condition tends to accrue can hit that ceiling. The memo treats the condition as a single hasOnly clause but a production governor condition is rarely that clean.
findings.evidence: Memo lines 156-163 enumerate four modifiedGrantsByRole limits but not the operator cap. Google IAM quotas: 'Logic operators in a role binding's condition expression: 12' (https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/quotas).
findings.suggested_fix: Note in the limits list that the governor's conditioned-setIamPolicy binding shares the 12-logic-operator ceiling with any other guards in the same condition, so the condition must stay simple (the no-&&/|| rule already pushes toward one hasOnly per binding — make that consequence explicit).

findings.title: SA-tags 'condition-usable' overstates what the Preview supports for grant-scoping
findings.location: memo §Orphan hazard — 'SA tags (tag bindings): key-value, listable, condition-usable'
findings.issue: Listing SA tags as 'condition-usable' alongside the orphan-marker job invites the reader to assume the tag could later feed an IAM condition that scopes grants (the memo elsewhere leans hard on conditions for authority). For the orphan-sweep the only properties that matter are key-value + listable, both of which the description sentinel also provides. 'Condition-usable' is true for tags generally as a resource-policy mechanism, but it is doing no work for the stated job and risks implying tags can scope the citizen grant model — which they cannot in the way modifiedGrantsByRole does, and SA tags remain Preview ('subject to Pre-GA Offerings Terms... as is').
findings.evidence: Memo line 142: 'SA tags (tag bindings): key-value, listable, condition-usable — the proper metadata mechanism.' The job stated is the orphan-sweep only (line 143: 'used only for the orphan-sweep'). Tags-for-SA Preview status confirmed at https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-accounts-tags.
findings.suggested_fix: Drop 'condition-usable' from the marker bullet (it is irrelevant to the orphan-sweep and over-promises), or qualify it explicitly as 'condition-usable in principle, not relied on here.' Keep the load-bearing properties: key-value + listable.

findings.title: Scaling ceiling of one-SA-per-person not bounded against project SA quota
findings.location: memo §Blast radius / §Scope and vocabulary (citizen = one SA per person)
findings.issue: The model's central structural choice is 'one citizen per person = one SA.' GCP caps service accounts per project (default quota, increasable on request, but finite and console-displayed per project). For the current keyfile/solo posture this is a non-issue, but the memo frames the citizen tier as the durable shared capability layer that federation plugs into — and the explicit virtue of federation is no per-person SA. The asymmetry (citizen tier consumes one project-SA-quota slot per operator; federate tier consumes none) is a real platform-imposed scaling difference between the two tiers that the 'structural isomorphism' framing glosses over.
findings.evidence: Memo lines 17-18, 187-191 (one SA per person, forced). Google IAM quotas lists service-accounts-per-project as a per-project quota ('Varies depending on the project'; https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/quotas). The memo's isomorphism claim (lines 33-38) names the identity layer as the only seam but does not surface this quota asymmetry as part of that seam.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to blast-radius or the isomorphism paragraph noting that citizen-tier identities consume a finite per-project SA quota whereas federates do not — a real (if distant) tier asymmetry beyond the resting-key difference already cited.

content.content: Structured output provided successfully

content.text:
GCP factual-accuracy review complete. I web-verified every platform claim the model rests on against current (2026) Google Cloud docs.

Verdict on the six target claims: all accurate.
- (a) SA tags Preview, no resource labels — confirmed (https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-accounts-tags).
- (b) `modifiedGrantsByRole`: project/folder/org only, ≤10 roles, no `&&`/`||` joining of `hasOnly`, setIamPolicy-roles excluded, service-recognition caveat — confirmed verbatim (https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/setting-limits-on-granting-roles).
- (c) `roles/artifactregistry.repoAdmin` carries `setIamPolicy` — confirmed circumstantially-but-strongly: the project's own RBSDK-director_invest.adoc grants the director repoAdmin and then has it run `getIamPolicy`/`setIamPolicy` on the AR repo, which only works if repoAdmin carries those permissions.
- (d) SA can exist with zero IAM bindings — confirmed (custom SAs receive no automatic roles).
- (e) federated principal → `principal://iam.googleapis.com/...` — confirmed (https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workload-identity-federation).
- (f) billing is a separate resource excluded by `roles/owner` — confirmed (https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/roles-permissions/billing); RBSGM-governor_mantle.adoc already grants owner "excluding billing permissions," so the codebase agrees.

The factual layer is unusually clean — no wrong/stale claims to report. My four findings are all platform facts the model SHOULD account for but doesn't (plus one imprecision), the highest-value being the 10-keys-per-SA quota against the new `rekey` verb's lifecycle.
